licenses.name: MIT License
licenses.description: Short and simple permissive license with only one requirement.

metadata.explanation_hint: Best for maximum adoption with minimal legal overhead.

licenses.name: Apache License 2.0
licenses.description: Permissive license with an explicit patent grant and protection against contributor patent claims.

metadata.explanation_hint: Best permissive choice when patent protection matters, e.g. corporate or hardware-adjacent projects.

licenses.name: GNU General Public License v3.0 only
licenses.description: Ensures all derivatives and distributed versions remain open source.

metadata.explanation_hint: Use when you want to guarantee all downstream modifications stay open.

licenses.name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0
licenses.description: Allows sharing and adaptation for non-commercial purposes only.

metadata.explanation_hint: Designed for creative content, not software. Prohibits commercial use.

licenses.name: GNU General Public License v2.0 only
licenses.description: Strong copyleft license that requires derivatives to be GPL-2.0; used by the Linux kernel.

metadata.explanation_hint: Historically significant (Linux kernel). Incompatible with Apache-2.0 and GPL-3.0 unless 'or later' clause is present. Use GPL-3.0 for new projects.

licenses.name: GNU General Public License v3.0 only
licenses.description: Strong copyleft with anti-tivoization and explicit patent grant; all derivatives must be GPL-3.0.

metadata.explanation_hint: Use when you want to guarantee all downstream modifications stay open AND want patent + tivoization protections. Incompatible with Apache-2.0 at distribution time.

licenses.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1 only
licenses.description: Allows proprietary software to link against the library without copyleft infection, but modifications to the library itself must be shared.

metadata.explanation_hint: Key nuance: dynamic linking is generally safe for proprietary apps; static linking may trigger copyleft depending on interpretation. Always clarify linking type.

licenses.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0 only
licenses.description: LGPL-2.1 terms plus GPL-3.0 additions (anti-tivoization, patent terms). Preferred for new libraries.

metadata.explanation_hint: Superset of LGPL-2.1 with GPL-3.0 protections. Use for libraries where you want wide adoption but still require library modifications to be shared.

licenses.name: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0
licenses.description: GPL-3.0 extended with a network-use (SaaS) copyleft clause: users who interact over a network must be given source access.

metadata.explanation_hint: The 'viral SaaS' license. If you run AGPL software as a web service, you must offer users the source. Ideal for open-core business models. Businesses often avoid it for internal tools.

licenses.name: Mozilla Public License 2.0
licenses.description: File-level copyleft: modified MPL files must be shared, but proprietary code in separate files can coexist.

metadata.explanation_hint: Middle ground between MIT and LGPL. Copyleft applies per-file, not per-project. Good for libraries where you want modifications shared but don't need full project copyleft. Compatible with GPL-2.0+.

licenses.name: BSD 2-Clause 'Simplified' License
licenses.description: Permissive license similar to MIT; requires attribution in source and binary distributions.

metadata.explanation_hint: Functionally near-identical to MIT. FreeBSD uses this. Slightly more formal phrasing. No patent grant.

licenses.name: BSD 3-Clause 'New' or 'Revised' License
licenses.description: BSD-2-Clause plus a non-endorsement clause prohibiting use of the project name for promotion without permission.

metadata.explanation_hint: Adds a non-endorsement clause vs BSD-2. Used by Django and many academic/research projects. No patent grant — consider Apache-2.0 for that.

licenses.name: ISC License
licenses.description: Functionally equivalent to MIT/BSD-2-Clause but shorter; default for many npm packages.

metadata.explanation_hint: Preferred by OpenBSD; common in Node.js ecosystem. Legally equivalent to MIT — choose whichever your ecosystem prefers.

licenses.name: The Unlicense
licenses.description: Dedicates software to the public domain; no restrictions whatsoever.

metadata.explanation_hint: Maximum freedom — no attribution required. Not valid in jurisdictions without a public domain concept. Consider CC0 as a safer alternative for cross-jurisdictional use.

licenses.name: Creative Commons Zero v1.0 Universal
licenses.description: Public domain dedication with fallback permissive license for jurisdictions that don't recognize public domain waivers.

metadata.explanation_hint: Preferred public domain tool for datasets and creative works. More legally robust than Unlicense internationally. Not OSI-approved (CC licenses are not designed for software).

licenses.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
licenses.description: Allows any use including commercial, with attribution required. Designed for creative works, not software.

metadata.explanation_hint: For documentation, datasets, media assets. NOT recommended for source code. OSI explicitly states CC licenses are not suitable for software.

licenses.name: Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International
licenses.description: Attribution required; derivatives must use the same or a compatible license (ShareAlike = copyleft for content).

metadata.explanation_hint: Wikipedia's license. 'Copyleft for content' — derivatives must also be CC-BY-SA. Not for software. Compatible with GPL-3.0 per Creative Commons.

licenses.name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International
licenses.description: Allows sharing and adaptation for non-commercial purposes only with attribution.

metadata.explanation_hint: For academic/creative work you want freely shared but not monetized. 'Commercial' is broadly interpreted — even indirect revenue can count. Not for software.

licenses.name: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International
licenses.description: Non-commercial use only; derivatives must use the same license. Most restrictive standard CC license.

metadata.explanation_hint: Widely used for educational materials and course content. Maximum control for non-commercial creators. Incompatible with most software licenses.

licenses.name: European Union Public Licence 1.2
licenses.description: Copyleft license designed for EU public sector software; legally valid across all EU jurisdictions. Compatible with several other copyleft licenses.

metadata.explanation_hint: Specifically designed for EU law. Compatible (at distribution) with GPL-2.0, GPL-3.0, AGPL-3.0, LGPL, MPL, CDDL, EPL. Good for government and public sector projects in Europe.

licenses.name: Eclipse Public License 2.0
licenses.description: File-level weak copyleft; modifications to EPL files must be shared. Allows combining with GPL-2.0+ under certain conditions.

metadata.explanation_hint: Used by Eclipse IDE, Jakarta EE, and many Java projects. Includes an explicit patent grant and a patent termination clause. Copyleft is per 'module', not per file.

licenses.name: Common Development and Distribution License 1.0
licenses.description: File-level copyleft from Sun Microsystems; used for OpenSolaris and ZFS. Modifications to covered files must be shared.

metadata.explanation_hint: Historically used by Sun/Oracle for ZFS and NetBeans. INCOMPATIBLE with GPL (FSF says so explicitly) — major source of controversy when Linux wanted ZFS. Not recommended for new projects.

licenses.name: Server Side Public License 1.0
licenses.description: MongoDB's license: if you offer the software as a service, you must open-source your entire service stack.

metadata.explanation_hint: Stronger than AGPL — SaaS providers must open their entire stack, not just the SSPL-covered component. OSI rejected it as not meeting the Open Source Definition. Used by MongoDB, Elasticsearch (older versions), Redis (older versions).

licenses.name: Business Source License 1.1
licenses.description: Source-available but not open source: restricts production use until a specified Change Date, after which a designated open-source license applies.

metadata.explanation_hint: Used by MariaDB, HashiCorp (Terraform), CockroachDB. NOT open source — it is 'source available'. Converts to an open-source license (e.g., Apache-2.0) after the Change Date (usually 4 years). Commercial production use requires a commercial license before that date.

licenses.name: Elastic License 2.0
licenses.description: Source-available license that prohibits providing the software as a managed service and circumventing license key restrictions.

metadata.explanation_hint: Used by Elasticsearch 7.11+ and Kibana. Allows internal and commercial use, but you CANNOT offer it as a SaaS/cloud service. Created in response to AWS re-licensing concerns. NOT open source by OSI definition.

licenses.name: Proprietary / All Rights Reserved
licenses.description: No rights granted beyond what the license explicitly states. Default copyright law applies — use, distribution, and modification require explicit permission.

metadata.explanation_hint: Default when no license is specified — all rights reserved by the author. Others cannot legally use, copy, modify, or distribute. Add a license file to allow any use.

licenses.name: Artistic License 2.0
licenses.description: Permissive with some conditions around redistributing modified versions; used by Perl and CPAN.

metadata.explanation_hint: Standard license for Perl/CPAN ecosystem. Allows proprietary modifications but requires renaming or marking them. Not widely used outside Perl.

licenses.name: Common Public Attribution License 1.0
licenses.description: MPL-based license with an added attribution/display requirement for user-facing applications.

metadata.explanation_hint: Used by SugarCRM. Requires displaying the original author's name/logo in the UI. FSF considers it non-free due to the attribution requirement being potentially onerous.

licenses.name: zlib License
licenses.description: Very permissive; modified versions must be marked as changed and cannot be misrepresented as original. Common in game development.

metadata.explanation_hint: Used by zlib, libpng, SDL. Unlike MIT, you must mark modified sources as changed. No attribution required in binaries — common in game engines and embedded systems.

licenses.name: PostgreSQL License
licenses.description: Short permissive license similar to MIT/BSD, used exclusively by the PostgreSQL project.

metadata.explanation_hint: Essentially MIT-equivalent. Used only by PostgreSQL. No practical reason to use it for new projects — choose MIT or BSD-2 instead.
